subject: [PATCH 3/4] mm: Fix demotion gfp by clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE

reviews.author: Andrew Morton
reviews.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton requested separation of stable fixes from cleanups and preparation against current mainline, as well as careful changelog description of userspace-visible runtime effects.
reviews.raw_body:
Please let's have the cc:stable fixes separated out from the cleanups,
and prepared against current -linus mainline.

Also, when proposing backportable fixes please ensure that the
changelogs carefully describe the userspace-visible runtime effects of
the bug.

Thanks.

reviews.author: Johannes Weiner
reviews.summary: Reviewer Johannes Weiner noted that the original patch's fix inadvertently disabled direct reclaim for THP allocations, making the request unnecessarily wimpy, and suggested a clearer comment to explain GFP_TRANSHUGE's own reclaim policy.
reviews.raw_body:
I don't think this is right.

The Fixes: did it this way to disable kswapd for THP allocations,
while still allowing the customary direct reclaim. Maybe a better
comment would have been: /* GFP_TRANSHUGE has its own reclaim policy */

After your fix, direct reclaim isn't allowed either, which makes the
request unnecessarily wimpy.

The Closes: refers to reclaim that should be avoided during demotion.
But if this path is taken during demotion it will already not recurse
into direct reclaim due to PF_MEMALLOC.

So I don't see a bug in the existing code. But maybe the comment could
be clearer.

reviews.author: David (Arm)
reviews.summary: Reviewer David requested that fixes be moved to the top of the patchset or sent independently
reviews.raw_body:
Also, please move fixes to the very beginning of the patch set, or
better, send them independently.

-- 
Cheers,

David

reviews.author: Gregory Price
reviews.summary: Reviewer Gregory Price noted that the GFP_RECLAIM flag is being masked and added back in multiple layers, making it difficult to reason about what the actual flags are at any given point.
reviews.raw_body:
The bigger issue how many times we see this particular flag getting
masked and apparently added back in at multiple layers. We saw two
or three paths (some unreachable) that can twiddle RECLAIM flags in
the stack for demotion (which is in reclaim already, so do the flags
matter?).

It makes it difficult to reason about what the GFP flags actually
are at any given point.

But yeah I wasn't sure to make of this code, it could be as you
suggested just a bad comment.

~Gregory

reviews.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that GFP_RECLAIM should be cleared after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE to maintain consistency with regular THP allocations and agreed to restructure in a future version (v2) to address the issue.

Author acknowledged a fix is needed to clear GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE and agreed to make the change.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Andrew,

On 3/11/26 18:06, Andrew Morton wrote:

---

I was unaware of that requirement, I'll do.

Thanks,

Alex

reviews.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that the GFP_RECLAIM flag must be cleared after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE to avoid inconsistent behavior, and agreed to restructure in a future version.

Author acknowledged that they misunderstood the original comment and agreed to restructure the patch in a future version.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Johannes,

On 3/11/26 18:54, Johannes Weiner wrote:

---

Makes sense, I had not understood the comment indeed. I'll drop this fix 
in the next version then.

Thanks,

Alex

2026-03-11.patch_summary: This patch fixes a demotion issue in the Linux kernel's memory management system by clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE. The problem was caused by an incorrect ordering of these flags, which led to inconsistent behavior between regular and transparent huge page (THP) allocations. The fix involves simply swapping the order of operations, ensuring that GFP_RECLAIM is cleared before GFP_TRANSHUGE is set.
